---
title: "[SOLVED] NTFS partitions do not automount after booting into windows"
date: 2008-04-16
forum: General Help
---

### Post by Blazed on 2008-04-16
Hi all,

I am dual booting Ubuntu and Vista. This is my fstab:

```
# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0

# /dev/sda1
UUID=2C0E9A580E9A1B42 /media/sda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_US.UTF-8 0 1

# /dev/sdb1
UUID=7616B2DF3BDC99DE /media/sdb1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_US.UTF-8 0 1

# /dev/sdc2
UUID=e602b0e4-fbd0-c79f-e96e-cf23f070957a / ext3 nouser,defaults,errors=remount-ro,atime,auto,rw,dev,exec,suid 0 0

# Entry for /dev/sdc5
UUID=b47bb702-20ef-4e22-9d42-e8558214bd49 none swap sw 0 0

/dev/hda /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto 0 0
/dev/sdc5 /home ext3 nodev,nosuid 0 2
```

For the past while everything was fine - the ntfs partitions always automounted (sda1 & sda2). Yesterday I booted into vista for a while and this morning when I booted ubuntu the ntfs partitions do not mount except for sdc1, which does.

blkid gives me:

```
/dev/sda1: TYPE="ntfs" UUID="2C0E9A580E9A1B42" LABEL="MUSHROOM1" 
/dev/sdb1: TYPE="ntfs" UUID="7616B2DF3BDC99DE" LABEL="MUSHROOM5" 
/dev/sdc1: TYPE="ntfs" UUID="4460C59A60C592DA" LABEL="MUSHROOM4" 
/dev/sdc2: UUID="a17265c6-b0de-ad8c-4849-b86d6e036bef" SEC_TYPE="ext2" TYPE="ext3" 
/dev/sdc5: LABEL="Home" UUID="e602b0e4-fbd0-c79f-e96e-cf23f070957a" SEC_TYPE="ext2" TYPE="ext3" 
/dev/sdc6: TYPE="swap" 

```

sudo fdisk -l gives me:

```

Disk /dev/sda: 80.0 GB, 80026361856 bytes
61 heads, 44 sectors/track, 58234 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 2684 * 512 = 1374208 bytes
Disk identifier: 0x0c64e550

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/sda1   *           1       58234    78148608    7  HPFS/NTFS

Disk /dev/sdb: 250.0 GB, 250059350016 bytes
255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 30401 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 16065 * 512 = 8225280 bytes
Disk identifier: 0x1a1edd85

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/sdb1               1       30401   244196001    7  HPFS/NTFS

Disk /dev/sdc: 251.0 GB, 251000193024 bytes
255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 30515 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 16065 * 512 = 8225280 bytes
Disk identifier: 0xbf3a45f4

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/sdc1               1       21279   170923536    7  HPFS/NTFS
/dev/sdc2   *       26501       30247    30097774   83  Linux
/dev/sdc3           21280       30515    74188170    5  Extended
/dev/sdc5           21280       26500    41937651   83  Linux
/dev/sdc6           30248       30515     2152678+  82  Linux swap / Solaris

Partition table entries are not in disk order

```

Please, can someone explain to me what the problem is / how to fix it?

](*,)

---

### Post by sisco311 on 2008-04-16
Try to mount manually:
```
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/sda1 -t ntfs-3g -o defaults,locale=en_US.UTF-8
```I think you didn't shut down Windows cleanly and the error message tells you to do it.

---

### Post by Blazed on 2008-04-16
Okay, so mounting manually does indeed tell me that windows did not shut down cleanly:

```
$LogFile indicates unclean shutdown (0, 0)
Failed to mount '/dev/sda1': Operation not supported
Mount is denied because NTFS is marked to be in use. Choose one action:

Choice 1: If you have Windows then disconnect the external devices by
          clicking on the 'Safely Remove Hardware' icon in the Windows
          taskbar then shutdown Windows cleanly.

Choice 2: If you don't have Windows then you can use the 'force' option for
          your own responsibility. For example type on the command line:

            mount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sda1 /media/sda1 -o force

    Or add the option to the relevant row in the /etc/fstab file:

            /dev/sda1 /media/sda1 ntfs-3g defaults,force 0 0

```

I am going to go do a clean shutdown then - thanks! Hopefully that resolves it.

---
